Measuring Congress in the AI Era:
What Modern Vote Prediction Reveals About
Members, Bills, and Institutions

Andrew B. Hall*

Draft: July 2026 — preliminary, comments welcome

Abstract
Nearly everything political science measures about members of Congress—ideology, loyalty, issue positions—is extracted from models that predict rollcall votes. Prediction quality therefore bounds measurement quality: a model that forecasts votes better has, by construction, captured more of the behavioral structure there is to measure. We build the strongest vote predictor to date—an ensemble reading rollcall-level text with modern language-model encoders, anchored on member histories, with calibrated probabilities (86.2% accuracy on strictly future votes; 85.1% on contested votes; verified prospectively on 9,451 votes cast after the model was cryptographically frozen)—and then use it as a measurement instrument. The model’s internals recover and extend the classical measurement canon: positions correlating at $r = .98$ with DW-NOMINATE, party-loyalty residuals that identify the chamber’s mavericks by name, issue-specific positions that recover libertarian non-interventionism from votes alone, bill-level cutpoints locating each rollcall on the ideological scale, and a new measure of out-of-character votes. Just as informative is what the instrument cannot do. Amendment coalitions resist text-based prediction because they form around deals rather than content—a measurable boundary on what legislative language reveals. And no model survives a change in majority control: member histories actively mislead across the 2022 House flip, showing that much of what vote models measure is institutional role, not stable preference. Career-spanning NOMINATE scores, by contrast, nearly match the deep model’s

*Stanford University. Replication materials, including the full pipeline, model registry, pre-registered experiment ledger, and session logs of the AI-assisted development process, are available in the project repository. Every statistic and figure in this paper is generated programmatically from the analysis code’s output files; no number is transcribed by hand.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

# Measuring Congress in the AI Era: What Modern Vote Prediction Reveals About Members, Bills, and Institutions

Andrew B. Hall*

Draft: July 2026 — preliminary, comments welcome

## Abstract

Nearly everything political science measures about members of Congress—ideology, loyalty, issue positions—is extracted from models that predict rollcall votes. Prediction quality therefore bounds measurement quality: a model that forecasts votes better has, by construction, captured more of the behavioral structure there is to measure. We build the strongest vote predictor to date—an ensemble reading rollcall-level text with modern language-model encoders, anchored on member histories, with calibrated probabilities (86.2% accuracy on strictly future votes; 85.1% on contested votes; verified prospectively on 9,451 votes cast after the model was cryptographically frozen)—and then use it as a measurement instrument. The model's internals recover and extend the classical measurement canon: positions correlating at $r = .98$ with DW-NOMINATE, party-loyalty residuals that identify the chamber's mavericks by name, issue-specific positions that recover libertarian non-interventionism from votes alone, bill-level cutpoints locating each rollcall on the ideological scale, and a new measure of out-of-character votes. Just as informative is what the instrument cannot do. Amendment coalitions resist text-based prediction because they form around deals rather than content—a measurable boundary on what legislative language reveals. And no model survives a change in majority control: member histories actively mislead across the 2022 House flip, showing that much of what vote models measure is institutional role, not stable preference. Career-spanning NOMINATE scores, by contrast, nearly match the deep model's

*Stanford University. Replication materials, including the full pipeline, model registry, pre-registered experiment ledger, and session logs of the AI-assisted development process, are available in the project repository. Every statistic and figure in this paper is generated programmatically from the analysis code's output files; no number is transcribed by hand.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

cross-congress accuracy—a genuine vindication of traditional measurement design. We release the benchmark, models, and a pre-registered ledger of all results, positive and negative.

## 1 Introduction

The empirical study of Congress rests on measurement, and the measurement rests on prediction. An ideal point is the parameter of a model built to predict votes *(Poole and Rosenthal, 1997; Clinton, Jackman and Rivers, 2004)*; a party-unity score is a prediction from party membership; an interest-group rating is a prediction from a hand-picked vote subset. The field’s measures of legislators are, almost without exception, the interpretable residue of vote-prediction machinery. This has a bracing implication that the measurement literature rarely states: how well the machinery predicts is an upper bound on how much the measures can know. Structure a model fails to predict is structure no measure derived from it can contain.

For four decades the machinery in question has been the spatial voting model, and it earned its dominance: one or two dimensions classify around ninety percent of votes in the modern Congress. The past fifteen years of scaling research has pushed on the machinery from every side: legislator-specific unpredictability *(Lauderdale, 2010)*, issue-specific dimensions estimated with topic-weighted votes *(Lauderdale and Clark, 2014; Crespin and Rohde, 2010)*, joint models of votes and speech *(Kim, Londregan and Ratkovic, 2018)*, computational estimators for massive data *(Imai, Lo and Olmsted, 2016)*, supervised learning as a measurement bridge *(Bonica, 2018)*, bill and status-quo locations for testing theories of lawmaking *(Peress, 2013; Richman, 2011)*, and, most recently, models built to rescue the members whom one-dimensional scaling visibly mis-measures—ideological extremists who vote with the opposite pole against the middle *(Duck-Mayr and Montgomery, 2023)* and majority members who cast protest votes against bills they prefer *(Fowler and Lewis, 2026)*. A parallel literature at the intersection of machine learning and legislative studies tried to read the bills themselves *(Gerrish and Blei, 2011, 2012; Kraft, Jain and Rush, 2016)*, but worked with the text tools of its era and went quiet before modern language models existed. Today’s encoders read legislative text incomparably better, and prediction bounds measurement. Do better predictors deliver richer, sharper, more trustworthy measures of Congress? What new things can we now measure? And where does even the best modern predictor hit a wall—and what do the walls teach us about the institution?

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

This paper answers in three movements. *First, we build and validate the instrument* (Section 2). On an open benchmark of 10.6 million voting decisions (101st–119th Congresses), we develop an ensemble model that reads rollcall-level text with sentence encoders, maps it to vote-choice parameters through deep heads, anchors on each member’s behavioral history, and reports calibrated probabilities. On strictly future votes within a congress it reaches 86.2% accuracy (85.1% on contested votes; log loss 0.324), far beyond both the classical spatial model and the 2011–2016 text-based models, which—under proper scoring rules and honest temporal splits—never actually beat a simple table of member voting rates (Table 1). Because a measurement instrument is only as credible as its evaluation, we score every model under four regimes of increasing difficulty and add a fifth test immune to researcher iteration by construction: models frozen and hash-pinned, then scored on the 9,451 votes Congress cast in the following weeks. The frozen model’s prospective performance (0.331 log loss, 85.7% accuracy) matches its development performance, certifying that the numbers here are not artifacts of adaptive overfitting.

*Second, we measure* (Sections 3 and 4). From the instrument’s internals we extract, and validate against external benchmarks, a suite of member- and bill-level measures. Learned member positions correlate with DW-NOMINATE’s first dimension at $r=$.98 (Figure 4)—the sanity check—and then go further. A *party-loyalty residual* isolates discipline from ideology and independently rediscovers the canonical mavericks of the last decade—Walter Jones, Justin Amash, Collin Peterson, Kyrsten Sinema—from votes alone (Figure 5). *Issue-specific positions* recover, without any text about the members, the libertarian non-interventionist bloc (dovish on defense, civil-libertarian on crime, otherwise very conservative) and the hawkish wing of the Democratic party (Figure 6). At the bill level, every rollcall acquires a *cutpoint*—the location on the ideological scale where a member becomes indifferent—and the distribution of cutpoints against the distribution of members maps the strategic structure of the agenda: which policy areas divide the parties at the center, which split one party internally, which pass by consensus (Figure 9). And because the model outputs calibrated probabilities for every vote, its largest errors constitute a new dataset of *out-of-character votes*—defections the best available behavioral model could not see coming (Figure 8)—a systematic starting point for studying pressure, persuasion, and idiosyncratic representation.

*Third, we measure the walls* (Section 5). Two boundaries survived every attempt we pre-registered against them, and both are substantive findings about Congress rather than engineering shortfalls. Amendment votes carry a third of the model’s remaining error, yet supplying amendments’ actual purpose text makes prediction *worse*, not better—

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

result we establish with a placebo-controlled design. Within a bill, the model exploits the correlation of the amendment series more effectively than the amendments’ content: amendment coalitions form around deals, not language. That is a measured boundary on what legislative text reveals—content organizes coalitions on final passage, context organizes them on amendments. Second, when majority control of the House changed in 2022, every history-based measure partially inverted: pooled member records *anti-transfer* across the flip (a member who reliably supported procedural motions in the majority reliably opposes them in the minority), our best model’s probabilities on the unseen congress are worse-calibrated than a three-way count table that merely knows who holds the chamber, and a simple logit on frozen DW-NOMINATE scores nearly matches the deep model’s transfer accuracy (62.0% versus 62.1%). The lesson is constitutional in the small: a large share of what any vote-based measure captures is institutional role, not stable preference, and measures built within one majority configuration should be carried across configurations only with care. That career-spanning NOMINATE scores—estimated from behavior in both roles—are the most durable object in our entire horse race is a quiet vindication of the traditional design.

Throughout, we practice the evaluation discipline the measurement stakes demand: every development experiment was pre-registered in a public ledger with hypothesis and falsification test written before the run, selection touched validation data only, the final model comparison observed test sets exactly once, and negative results are reported with their mechanisms. The benchmark, code, frozen artifacts, and ledger are public; a companion paper develops the member-level measures at full depth.

## 2 The Instrument: Prediction You Can Build Measures On

### 2.1 Data and task

We predict individual voting decisions: given everything observable before a rollcall, output each member’s probability of voting yea. The panel covers every yea/nay decision in the 101st through 119th Congresses from Voteview *(Lewis et al., 2026)*—10.6 million decisions—with bill-side information (titles, policy areas, dated summary versions, sponsors, amendment purposes) from government BILLSTATUS records from the 108th Congress forward. Rollcall-level text is assembled strictly from information available before each vote. Splits are deterministic and content-hashed; a common harness strips

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

|  Model | Log loss |   |   | Accuracy (%)  |   |   |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Constant rate | 0.659 | 0.617 | 0.693 | 63.0 | 70.9 | 57.1  |
|  Member × question rate | 0.494 | 0.459 | 0.693 | 72.1 | 76.0 | 57.1  |
|  Majority × question rate | 0.533 | 0.483 | 0.617 | 67.7 | 74.0 | 55.1  |
|  NOMINATE × context logit | 0.587 | 0.532 | 0.650 | 65.5 | 72.4 | 62.0  |
|  GB-style spatial (TF-IDF, calibrated) | 0.685 | 0.566 | 0.671 | 63.4 | 72.5 | 61.4  |
|  Kraft-style bilinear | 0.522 | 0.497 | 0.759 | 73.0 | 76.0 | 64.3  |

|  Three-tower blend (champion) | 0.390 | 0.324 | 0.643 | 81.0 | 86.2 | 62.1  |

labels before any model sees evaluation rows.

# 2.2 Why evaluation regimes are measurement questions

A model's fitness as a measurement instrument depends on what kind of prediction it is good at, so we evaluate under regimes of increasing difficulty: completion (random individual votes held out, same-rollcall votes observable—the psychometric setting in which ideal points are traditionally assessed); random rollcall holdout (whole rollcalls held out, interleaved in time—the 2011–2016 literature's setting); temporal forecast (the final ten percent of each congress-chamber's rollcalls—a true forecaster's setting); congress-out transfer (train through the 116th Congress, predict the 118th, across the majority flip); and prospective (hash-pinned frozen models scored only on votes that occur after freezing, following the logic of the reusable holdout problem (Dwork et al., 2015)). Our primary metric is log loss—a proper scoring rule that rewards honest probabilities—because measurement consumers use the probabilities, not just the classifications; accuracy, AUC, contested-vote accuracy, and APRE are reported throughout for comparability.

Table 1 and Figure 1 summarize the audit; three points matter for everything downstream. First, completion. We report it in the field's own statistics—correct classification, APRE, and geometric mean probability, the fit vocabulary of the NOMINATE tradition (Carroll et al., 2013); log loss is $-\log(\text{GMP})$, so our primary metric is the literature's in different units. An eight-dimensional model reaches GMP 0.858 (93.8% classification) on held-out votes against 0.849 for frozen DW-NOMINATE with estimated rollcall parameters (Appendix Table 6)—and the location of that margin is itself a finding. Figure 2

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)
Figure 1: The regime audit at a glance. Moving right, less about the vote's context is observable at prediction time. The 2011-2016 architectures never beat the member-history table in any true forecasting regime; the modern gains are large within a congress and vanish across the majority flip.

compares the two models member by member on identical held-out votes. For the typical member the classical model is nearly unimprovable (median GMP 0.858 versus 0.863; ours is higher for  $67.1\%$  of members); the large gains concentrate in precisely the members the past decade of scaling research built bespoke models to rescue—Ron Paul (GMP  $0.27 \rightarrow 0.61$ ), Justin Amash, Thomas Massie, Dennis Kucinich: the ends-against-the-middle and protest voters of Duck-Mayr and Montgomery (2023) and Fowler and Lewis (2026), recovered here by one flexible model rather than phenomenon-specific likelihoods. Convergent validity—the discipline of checking a new measure against every external benchmark available (Shor and McCarty, 2011)—holds throughout: per-congress positions correlate with Nokken-Poole scores (Nokken and Poole, 2004) at  $r = 0.96 - 0.98$  across recent chamber-congresses. Still, completion-regime performance should never be quoted as "prediction."

The 2011-2016 text models, re-implemented faithfully and given modern courtesies (Appendix Table 7), fail under proper scoring: the Gerrish-Blei-style model's raw forecast log loss (0.803) is worse than predicting the base rate—severe miscalibration invisible to the accuracy metrics of the original papers (Figure 3)—and even after temperature repair it trails a member-by-question count table. And the modern stack clears everything within a congress by a wide margin while hitting the same wall as everything else across congresses (Section 5).

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-1.jpeg](img-1.jpeg)
Figure 2: Member-by-member geometric mean probability on identical held-out completion-regime votes: this paper's spatial model against the classical model (frozen DW-NOMINATE positions, estimated rollcall parameters). Members above the diagonal fit better under the richer model; the largest gains (labeled) are the literature's canonical hard cases. Blue: Democrats; red: Republicans.

# 2.3 The champion and its certification

The final model is a logistic blend of three two-tower networks. Each tower embeds members in a latent space and amortizes each rollcall's choice parameters from its text—the vote question, the vote description, and the latest pre-vote bill summary, encoded by modern sentence encoders (the three towers vary the encoder and text representation)—anchored by a member-by-question-type empirical rate and calibrated on a temporal internal slice of training data. Development followed a pre-registered ledger; the ablation path and the full set of negative results (bigger encoders, recency weighting, within-bill context features, per-bucket calibration) are in Appendix C with their diagnosed mechanisms.

On the temporal forecast test—689,351 future votes—the blend reaches 0.324 log loss,  $86.2\%$  accuracy,  $85.1\%$  contested accuracy, APRE 0.48. For certification beyond any possibility of test-set contamination: a predecessor model frozen on June 12, 2026 (weights hash-pinned, fit through June 9) scored 0.331 log loss and  $85.7\%$  accuracy on the 43 rollcalls and 9,451 votes Congress cast June 10–30—statistically indistinguishable

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-2.jpeg](img-2.jpeg)
Figure 3: Calibration on strictly future rollcalls. The 2011-style model's probabilities are badly overconfident—the failure mode that accuracy metrics cannot see and that makes uncalibrated model output unusable as measurement.

Table 2: The prospective ledger: frozen, hash-pinned artifacts scored only on votes cast after each model's data snapshot.

|  Artifact | Snapshot | Rollcalls | Votes | Log loss | Acc. (%) | AUC  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  v1 (emb2-MLP tower) | 2026-06-09 | 43 | 9,451 | 0.331 | 85.7 | 0.924  |
|  v2 (three-tower blend) | 2026-06-09 | 43 | 9,451 | 0.332 | 86.8 | 0.921  |

from its development test numbers (Table 2). The blend, frozen July 3, has begun its own prospective clock. Measurement built on this instrument inherits that certification.

# 3 Measuring Members

# 3.1 Positions, validated

The instrument's member representations should first reproduce what we already trust. Fitting the benchmark's one-dimensional spatial model per chamber (with the identification conventions documented in the repository), learned positions correlate with DW-NOMINATE's first dimension at  $r = .98$  in both chambers—and at .84-.91 within party, where the information is (Figure 4). Nothing about DW-NOMINATE entered the estimation; the agreement is two instruments discovering the same object.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

![img-3.jpeg](img-3.jpeg)
Figure 4: Validation: positions learned by the benchmark's spatial model (vertical axis) against DW-NOMINATE (horizontal), by chamber. Blue: Democrats; red: Republicans.

![img-4.jpeg](img-4.jpeg)

# 3.2 Loyalty beyond ideology

Ideology and discipline are conceptually distinct—a centrist who always answers the whip differs from a centrist who defects—but one-dimensional scores conflate them. Using the instrument, we compute each member's expected behavior on party-unity votes given their position, and define the loyalty residual as actual minus expected unity-vote support. Figure 5 shows the complete recent-era distribution—every member, not a curated list—and its two systematic properties. The distribution is tight and asymmetric: most members are within a point or two of their position-predicted loyalty, with a long defector tail and almost no excess-loyalty tail, indicating that party pressure binds nearly everyone to a common ceiling while defection is where the individual variation lives. And the residual is essentially uncorrelated with ideological extremity ( $\rho = -.05$ ): the measure captures a dimension of behavior that position does not, which is precisely its purpose. The labeled extremes—who emerge from the full distribution rather than from selection—are the members journalists and whips would name: Walter Jones as the era's outstanding maverick, then Justin Amash, Jeff Van Drew (measured before his party switch—the residual saw it coming), Collin Peterson. The residual is discipline net of preference—the quantity party-unity scores are often mistaken for.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Party loyalty beyond ideology, 115th-119th Congresses

![img-5.jpeg](img-5.jpeg)
Figure 5: The loyalty residual for every member serving in the 115th-119th Congresses (at least 100 party-unity votes; territorial delegates excluded). Left: the full distribution by party. Right: the residual against the member's ideal point, with the most extreme residuals labeled. Blue: Democrats; red: Republicans.

![img-6.jpeg](img-6.jpeg)

# 3.3 Issue-specific positions

Issue-specific structure has a distinguished lineage—appropriations votes scale differently from the rest of the agenda (Crespin and Rohde, 2010), and topic-weighted estimation recovers issue variation in courts and Congress (Lauderdale and Clark, 2014)—but coverage has been the constraint. Because every rollcall here carries a policy area from its bill's text metadata, the spatial model can be re-estimated within policy areas, giving each member a position on economics, defense, crime, health, and ten other domains—the structure a single dimension is forced to average away. Coverage is essentially complete: between roughly 800 and 1,400 members receive a position in every major area (fifty-vote minimum per member-area), and Table 3 reports the full per-topic accounting.

Three systematic facts emerge. First, one dimension is almost sufficient—within-area positions correlate with overall positions at  $\rho = .95 - .99$  (Figure 6)—which is precisely why the deviations that remain are informative rather than noise: they are the structured residue that survives a near-perfect one-dimensional fit. Second, polarization is not uniform across the agenda (Figure 7). The parties stand farthest apart on energy and taxation—the modern economic core—and closest together on armed forces and international affairs, which are also the areas with the widest within-party spreads: foreign policy is simultaneously the least partisan and the most internally contested terrain in Congress,

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

in both parties. Third, the deviations recover known ideological factions from votes alone, systematically rather than anecdotally. Reading down Table 3's largest recent-era deviators: the liberal-direction outliers on armed forces and international affairs are the libertarian Republicans (Paul, Crane, Roy, Sanford), civil-libertarian deviations appear on crime (Brat, Burlison), and hawkish or border-district Democrats deviate conservatively in the same areas. None of these members' texts, speeches, or biographies entered the estimation; their votes alone locate them.

# 3.4 Out-of-character votes

A calibrated model of member behavior defines, vote by vote, what would be surprising—and its largest realized surprises are a dataset no existing measure provides. Figure 8 shows the decisions the frozen champion, fit on the member's entire record, still got confidently wrong. These are not model noise; inspected, they are recognizable episodes—high-pressure defections, district-driven crossovers, protest votes—now identifiable systematically rather than anecdotally. We release the full surprise ranking as a research resource: a sampling frame for studying lobbying, leadership pressure, and the votes that end careers.

Two clarifications locate this measure in the literature's lineage. Relative to legislator-

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

specific variance in a one-dimensional model *(Lauderdale, 2010)*, our unpredictability is *residual to a far richer specification*: behavior must escape text, issue structure, party discipline, and the member’s own detailed history before it registers as surprising. And relative to protest voting *(Fowler and Lewis, 2026)*: the Squad’s protest pattern is *regular*—a rich model learns it, which is exactly why those members show ordinary loyalty residuals and no excess presence in our surprise tail, while a one-dimensional model registers them as its worst-fit members (Figure 2). What the classical framework treats as measurement failure is, to a sufficiently expressive model, simply another behavioral signature; what remains in *our* surprise tail is the genuinely episodic residue.

## 4 Measuring Bills

The same machinery measures the *agenda*. Every rollcall acquires a discrimination (how strongly it separates the space) and a cutpoint—the position of an indifferent member. Figure 9 plots the 118th House: the member distribution is bimodal and the cutpoint distribution concentrates in the empty center—the signature of an agenda organized to divide the parties—but the policy-area breakdown shows the exceptions in measurable form: areas whose median cutpoint sits inside a party (internal factional fights) and areas whose cutpoints sit at the extremes (consensus votes that separate only the flanks).

Cutpoint locations relative to the chamber’s pivotal actors are the raw material of the agenda-control literature *(Richman, 2011; Peress, 2013; Aldrich, Montgomery and Sparks, 2014)*, and the full-agenda measurement makes the standard quantities immediate. In the 118th House, only 15.0% of the 984 identified cutpoints fall between the floor median (0.48) and the majority-party median (1.77)—the region where a vote splits the majority party against its own median—while 69.3% fall to the *left* of the floor median, where the Republican conference votes together against most Democrats. That asymmetry—an agenda engineered to divide the opposition rather than the majority—is negative agenda control rendered as a histogram, computed from every recorded vote of the congress rather than a curated subset.

Text enters the measurement here: the model’s text-to-parameter head is a learned map from legislative language to conflict structure, and its successes and failures delineate what bill content *is* to a coalition. On final-passage votes, text carries real information—the ablation path in Appendix Table 8 shows rollcall-level text construction contributing some of the largest gains. On amendments, Section 5 shows the opposite, and the contrast is itself the measurement: language predicts coalitions where coalitions form on

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

content, and the places it fails are the places procedure and bargaining, not content, do the organizing.

### 4.1 Predicting where a bill will cut, from its text

The dashboard’s premise—that a bill’s language locates it on the scale before anyone votes—can be tested directly, and Table 4 and Figure 10 do so. For every rollcall in the benchmark we take the realized cutpoint and direction (which party’s side the yeas fall on) from the spatial fits, hold out the final twenty percent of each congress-chamber’s rollcalls, and ask how well features available *before* the vote predict them: nothing; metadata (question type, sponsor party, bill category); text alone (in classical and modern representations); or text and metadata together. On 3,549 held-out future rollcalls, text plus metadata predicts cutpoint locations with a mean error of 0.64 member-standard-deviations ($r=0.55$, against 0.86 with no features) and calls the coalition’s direction correctly 74.5% of the time (chance: 55.8%).

The decomposition is as informative as the headline, and we report the metadata baseline at full strength, which took two rounds of honest reinforcement. A bill’s sponsor is directional for passage votes (the yeas fall on the sponsor’s side 82 percent of the time) but a coin flip for amendment votes, where the relevant actor is the *amender*—frequently the other party attacking the bill. The final metadata model therefore carries both actors’ parties (bill sponsor everywhere; amender’s party on the roughly three-quarters of amendment votes recoverable from amendment records, where the amender-side rule alone calls direction at 77 percent), sponsor-by-question interactions, question type, bill category, and chamber. So strengthened, metadata alone is formidable: it locates cutpoints nearly as well as anything (MAE = 0.66; *where* a vote cuts is substantially institutional) and calls direction at 70.8%. Text still earns its keep on every metric: direction rises to 74.5% and the correlation of predicted with realized locations from $r=0.48$ to 0.55. Among the text’s components, the bill summary carries the most directional signal and the vote question the most locational signal (Table 4, bottom panel). The synthesis: institutions pin where a vote cuts, the acting legislator’s party supplies a default side, and content is what tells you when the default is wrong—and by how much.

### 4.2 The language of left- and right-recruiting bills

What in the text carries the directional signal? Fitting an interpretable sparse model (TF–IDF terms, final-passage votes, identified rollcalls, alphabetic tokens only) exposes

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Table 4: Predicting held-out future rollcalls' cutpoints and coalition directions from pre-vote features. Cutpoint error in member-standard-deviation units, identified rollcalls; direction accuracy on identified rollcalls. Bottom panel: text components separately (TF-IDF pipeline).

|  Features | Cutpoint MAE | Cutpoint r | Direction acc. (%)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Constant | 0.856 | 0.00 | 55.8  |
|  Metadata only | 0.657 | 0.48 | 70.8  |
|  Text: TF-IDF | 0.742 | 0.43 | 61.3  |
|  Text: embeddings | 0.735 | 0.42 | 59.5  |
|  Text + metadata | 0.637 | 0.55 | 74.5  |
|  question only | 0.763 | 0.41 | 58.2  |
|  description only | 0.813 | 0.25 | 57.2  |
|  bill summary only | 0.765 | 0.38 | 59.7  |

the coefficients directly, and Figure 11 shows the result: a lexicon of coalition recruitment. Bills whose summaries establish programs, authorize assistance, and provide grants and services to workers and employees recruit liberal-yea coalitions; bills about rules, regulations, permits, budgets, statutory revision (sec, code, amends), and injury (the tort-reform lexicon) recruit conservative-yea ones. The map is, recognizably, the administrative state built versus the administrative state checked—estimated from votes and bill summaries alone. We reached this exhibit through three pre-registered face-validity corrections worth recording, because each is a warning to text-as-data practice: naive fits are dominated first by year tokens (which proxy for who held the majority—agenda composition, not language), then by procedural boilerplate from rules resolutions; only after restricting to substantive passage-vote summaries does bill language itself speak.

Because the map from text to conflict structure is an estimated object, it can be queried with bills that do not exist. We release, alongside the paper, an interactive forecaster: paste the text of a hypothetical bill, name a sponsoring party and chamber, and the frozen model returns a predicted cutpoint, party-by-party support, and each current member's vote probability (Appendix B). The tool exposes the instrument's boundaries as deliberately as its capabilities: because floor agendas are curated by the majority party, the model has never observed votes on off-agenda bills, and the dashboard flags forecasts whose internal structure reveals extrapolation—for instance, when the predicted coalition contradicts the bill's own sponsorship. The dashboard is thus both a research tool and a live demonstration of the agenda-selection boundary discussed above.

# 5 Measuring the Limits—and What They Say About Congress

# 5.1 Amendments: deals, not content

Amendment votes carry roughly thirty percent of the instrument's remaining error (Table 5), and two-thirds of amendment rollcalls lack amendment-specific text—so supplying the amendments' actual purpose language, parsed from BILLSTATUS records and joined through recorded-vote references, looked like the obvious improvement. Pre-registered, it failed three ways, and the failure is the finding. Corrupted joins, corrected joins, and minimal-footprint text insertions all made prediction worse by similar amounts; a placebo rebuild that changed the data file without changing content reproduced the champion exactly, exonerating the pipeline. Mechanism: when a bill's amendments share the parent bill's text, the model assigns the series nearly identical parameters—an implicit pooled estimate of a correlated block. Purpose language replaces that pooling with per-amendment, content-driven parameters, and content predicts amendment coalitions worse than the bill's identity does. What organizes amendment voting is the deal context—who offered it, against which bill, at which procedural moment—not what the amendment says. This is a measured boundary of the text-as-data program in legislatures, and it corroborates, at the micro level of individual votes, accounts of amendment politics as coalition management.

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

5.2 Majority control: role, not preference

The 118th Congress column of Table 1 is the deepest lesson. Trained through 2021 and asked about 2023–24—across the House majority flip—the champion’s probabilities are worse-calibrated than a count table keyed only on majority status, chamber, and question type (0.643 versus 0.617); pooled member histories are outright poisonous (log loss 0.740), because the member who reliably supported procedural motions as a majority member reliably opposes them in the minority. A synthetic two-world experiment in the appendix isolates the mechanism: where behavior is majority-role-driven, member history anti-transfers exactly as observed; where behavior is a stable trait, it transfers nearly perfectly. Real congressional voting is a blend, and on the procedural votes that fill the calendar, the role component dominates. Attempts to repair the model with majority-status features fail for an identifiable reason—within a congress, member history is strictly more informative, so the model never learns to use the features that would survive the flip. Meanwhile the logit on frozen, career-spanning DW-NOMINATE scores nearly matches the deep model’s transfer accuracy (62.0% versus 62.1%).

For measurement practice the implications are direct, and they extend a caution the American political development literature has pressed on other grounds: cross-time comparisons of vote-based scores are entangled with changes in what the chamber votes on *(Bateman et al., 2017)*. Vote-derived scores estimated within one majority configuration partly measure that configuration; comparisons of member scores across a change in control confound preference change with role change; and the durability of career-spanning estimation—long derided as an assumption of implausible preference stability—looks instead like insurance against role contamination. For the benchmark, cross-configuration transfer is the posted challenge: no model we tested, and none we could design under pre-registration, solves it.

## 6 Conclusion

Better prediction has made Congress more measurable: positions that match the classical canon, loyalty separated from ideology, issue positions recovered from votes alone, an agenda mapped bill by bill, and a systematic catalog of the votes no model saw coming. It has also made the limits measurable, and they are not incidental: legislative language organizes some coalitions and not others, and a large share of observed voting behavior belongs to institutional role rather than to the member. Instruments, measures, and their documented boundaries are released together—including a frozen model whose forecasting

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

claims the future itself will continue to audit.

# A Additional Tables

# B The Interactive Forecaster

The dashboard wraps the frozen champion's MiniLM component tower (the blend's TF-IDF component requires corpus-level context a single pasted bill lacks; the MiniLM tower alone carries the largest blend weight). Pasted text is assembled into the training text template and embedded with the training encoder; a synthetic bill record carries the user-specified sponsoring party through the same features used in training; member vote probabilities are computed for the full current roster; and the displayed cutpoint is the position at which predicted support crosses one half, from a logistic fit of predicted votes on the current congress's estimated positions—mirroring the paper's cutpoint estimation

on realized votes. Sanity behavior: a commemorative post-office naming yields near-unanimous predicted support and no identified cutpoint; a sponsor-aligned tax bill yields a party-line forecast with a center-left cutpoint. Off-agenda hypotheticals trigger the extrapolation flag described in the text. The service is a single CPU process suitable for a small container host; deployment configuration accompanies the repository.

# C The Experiment Ledger

Every development experiment was pre-registered in the repository's ledger before its first run, with a hypothesis, a pre-vote-knowability argument for any new feature, and a falsification test. Selection used validation sets only; the three-model final comparison observed the test sets once. The ledger records, with diagnosed mechanisms: the encoder-scale negative (a  $16 \times$  larger text budget does not improve forecasts and worsens long-bill votes); the amendment-text negatives and their placebo control (Section 5); the recency-weighting negative (decayed member rates add estimator noise exceeding any drift signal); the within-bill context negative (features that pass synthetic tests at the information-theoretic optimum destabilize joint training on real data); the per-bucket calibration negative; and the regime-inheritance result (post-hoc parameters fit on a temporal development slice transfer to temporal evaluation and break on interleaved evaluation). Full reproduction instructions accompany the repository.
